For any fixed scene ss, any fixed adversarial environment distribution νs∈Δ​(Ωs⋆)\nu_{s}\in\Delta(\Omega_{s}^{\star}), and any two planners π,π′\pi,\pi^{\prime}, the finite-horizon utility satisfies

|Js​(π,νs)−Js​(π′,νs)|≤ΛH,γ​δ​(π,π′),\big|J_{s}(\pi,\nu_{s})-J_{s}(\pi^{\prime},\nu_{s})\big|\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\,\delta(\pi,\pi^{\prime}), (41)

where

δ(π,π′):=supxDTV(π(⋅∣x),π′(⋅∣x)).\delta(\pi,\pi^{\prime}):=\sup_{x}D_{\mathrm{TV}}\big(\pi(\cdot\mid x),\pi^{\prime}(\cdot\mid x)\big). (42)

Consequently, for every π∈Πϵ\pi\in\Pi_{\epsilon},

|Js​(π,νs)−Js​(πref,νs)|≤ΛH,γ​δϵ.\big|J_{s}(\pi,\nu_{s})-J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\nu_{s})\big|\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. (43)

Proof. Couple the two rollouts of π\pi and π′\pi^{\prime} against the same sampled environment law ω∼νs\omega\sim\nu_{s} and the same exogenous randomness. Conditional on identical prefixes up to a given step, the two rollouts have the same state and therefore the same non-ego conditional laws; we couple the non-ego samples identically until the ego token first differs. As long as the generated token prefixes are identical, the scene states are identical because the transition operator Γ\Gamma is deterministic given the sampled token set. At any step tt, conditional on identical prefixes up to t−1t-1, a maximal coupling of the ego token distributions gives

ℙ(ze,t≠ze,t′)≤DTV(π(⋅∣xt),π′(⋅∣xt))≤δ(π,π′).\mathbb{P}(z_{e,t}\neq z^{\prime}_{e,t})\leq D_{\mathrm{TV}}\big(\pi(\cdot\mid x_{t}),\pi^{\prime}(\cdot\mid x_{t})\big)\leq\delta(\pi,\pi^{\prime}). (44)

Let EtE_{t} be the event that the two coupled rollouts have diverged at or before step tt. By the union bound,

ℙ​(Et)≤t​δ​(π,π′).\mathbb{P}(E_{t})\leq t\,\delta(\pi,\pi^{\prime}). (45)

On EtcE_{t}^{c}, the two-step utilities are identical. On EtE_{t}, their difference is at most 2​umax2u_{\max}. Therefore,

|Js​(π,νs)−Js​(π′,νs)|\displaystyle\big|J_{s}(\pi,\nu_{s})-J_{s}(\pi^{\prime},\nu_{s})\big| ≤∑t=1Hγt−1⋅2​umax​ℙ​(Et)\displaystyle\leq\sum_{t=1}^{H}\gamma^{t-1}\cdot 2u_{\max}\mathbb{P}(E_{t}) (46)
≤2​umax​∑t=1Ht​γt−1​δ​(π,π′)=ΛH,γ​δ​(π,π′).\displaystyle\leq 2u_{\max}\sum_{t=1}^{H}t\gamma^{t-1}\delta(\pi,\pi^{\prime})=\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta(\pi,\pi^{\prime}).

Eq. 43 follows from Eq. 40. □\square

C.2 Local Stackelberg Justification of the Decoupled Stage-A/B Solver

We now analyze the error introduced by freezing Stage A before updating the planner. The ideal adversary class Δ​(Ωs⋆)\Delta(\Omega_{s}^{\star}) is too large to optimize directly. Stage A restricts it to the structured class induced by the AWM pipeline: proposal→primary selector→conditional pair ranker→scene calibrator\texttt{proposal}\;\rightarrow\;\texttt{primary selector}\;\rightarrow\;\texttt{conditional pair ranker}\;\rightarrow\;\texttt{scene calibrator}.

Let Ω^s⊆Ωs⋆\widehat{\Omega}_{s}\subseteq\Omega_{s}^{\star} denote the support covered by this structured pipeline, and define 𝒬s⋆\mathcal{Q}_{s}^{\star} as the full set of admissible adversarial environment distributions satisfying the attack budget and realism constraints. Let 𝒬^s⊆𝒬s⋆\widehat{\mathcal{Q}}_{s}\subseteq\mathcal{Q}_{s}^{\star} denote the structured class achieved by our AWM pipeline:

𝒬^s:=Δ​(Ω^s)⊆𝒬s⋆:=Δ​(Ωs⋆).\widehat{\mathcal{Q}}_{s}:=\Delta(\widehat{\Omega}_{s})\subseteq\mathcal{Q}_{s}^{\star}:=\Delta(\Omega_{s}^{\star}). (47)

For a fixed planner π\pi, define the full and structured worst-case utilities:

gs(π):=infνs∈𝒬s⋆Js(π,νs),g^s(π):=infνs∈𝒬^sJs(π,νs).g_{s}(\pi):=\inf_{\nu_{s}\in\mathcal{Q}_{s}^{\star}}J_{s}(\pi,\nu_{s}),\qquad\widehat{g}_{s}(\pi):=\inf_{\nu_{s}\in\widehat{\mathcal{Q}}_{s}}J_{s}(\pi,\nu_{s}). (48)

Because 𝒬^s⊆𝒬s⋆\widehat{\mathcal{Q}}_{s}\subseteq\mathcal{Q}_{s}^{\star}, we have

g^s​(π)≥gs​(π).\widehat{g}_{s}(\pi)\geq g_{s}(\pi). (49)

The structured class may not cover the full adversarial class. We define the scene-level coverage gap as

δcov,s​(π):=g^s​(π)−gs​(π)≥0.\delta_{\mathrm{cov},s}(\pi):=\widehat{g}_{s}(\pi)-g_{s}(\pi)\geq 0. (50)
Proposition C.2 (Local error of freezing the Stage-A adversary)

Assume Stage A returns a frozen structured adversary ν^s∈𝒬^s\widehat{\nu}_{s}\in\widehat{\mathcal{Q}}_{s} satisfying the reference-planner suboptimality condition

Js​(πref,ν^s)≤g^s​(πref)+εA,s.J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\widehat{\nu}_{s})\leq\widehat{g}_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})+\varepsilon_{A,s}. (51)

Then, for every planner π∈Πϵ\pi\in\Pi_{\epsilon},

0≤Js​(π,ν^s)−gs​(π)≤εA,s+δcov,s​(πref)+2​ΛH,γ​δϵ.0\leq J_{s}(\pi,\widehat{\nu}_{s})-g_{s}(\pi)\leq\varepsilon_{A,s}+\delta_{\mathrm{cov},s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})+2\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. (52)

Proof. The lower bound follows immediately from the definition of gs​(π)g_{s}(\pi):

gs​(π)=infνs∈𝒬s⋆Js​(π,νs)≤Js​(π,ν^s).g_{s}(\pi)=\inf_{\nu_{s}\in\mathcal{Q}_{s}^{\star}}J_{s}(\pi,\nu_{s})\leq J_{s}(\pi,\widehat{\nu}_{s}). (53)

For the upper bound, add and subtract the reference-planner quantities:

Js​(π,ν^s)−gs​(π)\displaystyle J_{s}(\pi,\widehat{\nu}_{s})-g_{s}(\pi) =Js​(π,ν^s)−Js​(πref,ν^s)⏟(I)+Js​(πref,ν^s)−gs​(πref)⏟(I​I)+gs​(πref)−gs​(π)⏟(I​I​I).\displaystyle=\underbrace{J_{s}(\pi,\widehat{\nu}_{s})-J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\widehat{\nu}_{s})}_{(I)}+\underbrace{J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\widehat{\nu}_{s})-g_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})}_{(II)}+\underbrace{g_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-g_{s}(\pi)}_{(III)}.

By Lemma C.1,

(I)≤ΛH,γ​δϵ.(I)\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. (54)

For term (I​I)(II), Eq. 51 and Eq. 50 give

(I​I)\displaystyle(II) =Js​(πref,ν^s)−gs​(πref)\displaystyle=J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\widehat{\nu}_{s})-g_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}) (55)
≤εA,s+g^s​(πref)−gs​(πref)\displaystyle\leq\varepsilon_{A,s}+\widehat{g}_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-g_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})
=εA,s+δcov,s​(πref).\displaystyle=\varepsilon_{A,s}+\delta_{\mathrm{cov},s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}).

For term (I​I​I)(III), observe that gs​(π)g_{s}(\pi) is the pointwise infimum of functions Js​(π,νs)J_{s}(\pi,\nu_{s}) that are all ΛH,γ\Lambda_{H,\gamma}-Lipschitz in π\pi by Lemma C.1. The pointwise infimum of uniformly Lipschitz functions is also Lipschitz with the same constant. Thus

(I​I​I)≤|gs​(πref)−gs​(π)|≤ΛH,γ​δϵ.(III)\leq|g_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-g_{s}(\pi)|\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. (56)

Combining the bounds on (I)(I), (I​I)(II), and (I​I​I)(III) proves Eq. 52. □\square

Corollary C.3 (Approximate local robust value guarantee)

Define the ideal local robust value

Vϵ⋆:=supπ∈Πϵ𝔼s∼𝒟​[gs​(π)].V_{\epsilon}^{\star}:=\sup_{\pi\in\Pi_{\epsilon}}\mathbb{E}_{s\sim\mathcal{D}}\big[g_{s}(\pi)\big]. (57)

Let Stage B return a planner π^∈Πϵ\widehat{\pi}\in\Pi_{\epsilon} that is an εB\varepsilon_{B}-approximate best response to the frozen Stage-A adversary:

𝔼s​[Js​(π^,ν^s)]≥supπ∈Πϵ𝔼s​[Js​(π,ν^s)]−εB.\mathbb{E}_{s}[J_{s}(\widehat{\pi},\widehat{\nu}_{s})]\geq\sup_{\pi\in\Pi_{\epsilon}}\mathbb{E}_{s}[J_{s}(\pi,\widehat{\nu}_{s})]-\varepsilon_{B}. (58)

Let

ε¯A:=𝔼s​[εA,s],δ¯cov:=𝔼s​[δcov,s​(πref)].\bar{\varepsilon}_{A}:=\mathbb{E}_{s}[\varepsilon_{A,s}],\qquad\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}:=\mathbb{E}_{s}[\delta_{\mathrm{cov},s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})]. (59)

Then

𝔼s​[gs​(π^)]≥Vϵ⋆−εB−ε¯A−δ¯cov−2​ΛH,γ​δϵ.\mathbb{E}_{s}[g_{s}(\widehat{\pi})]\geq V_{\epsilon}^{\star}-\varepsilon_{B}-\bar{\varepsilon}_{A}-\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}-2\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. (60)

Proof. Taking expectation in Proposition C.2 yields, for every π∈Πϵ\pi\in\Pi_{\epsilon},

𝔼s​[gs​(π)]≥𝔼s​[Js​(π,ν^s)]−ε¯A−δ¯cov−2​ΛH,γ​δϵ.\mathbb{E}_{s}[g_{s}(\pi)]\geq\mathbb{E}_{s}[J_{s}(\pi,\widehat{\nu}_{s})]-\bar{\varepsilon}_{A}-\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}-2\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. (61)

Applying Eq. 61 at π=π^\pi=\widehat{\pi} and using Eq. 58,

𝔼s​[gs​(π^)]\displaystyle\mathbb{E}_{s}[g_{s}(\widehat{\pi})] ≥𝔼s​[Js​(π^,ν^s)]−ε¯A−δ¯cov−2​ΛH,γ​δϵ\displaystyle\geq\mathbb{E}_{s}[J_{s}(\widehat{\pi},\widehat{\nu}_{s})]-\bar{\varepsilon}_{A}-\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}-2\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon} (62)
≥supπ∈Πϵ𝔼s​[Js​(π,ν^s)]−εB−ε¯A−δ¯cov−2​ΛH,γ​δϵ\displaystyle\geq\sup_{\pi\in\Pi_{\epsilon}}\mathbb{E}_{s}[J_{s}(\pi,\widehat{\nu}_{s})]-\varepsilon_{B}-\bar{\varepsilon}_{A}-\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}-2\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}
≥supπ∈Πϵ𝔼s​[gs​(π)]−εB−ε¯A−δ¯cov−2​ΛH,γ​δϵ\displaystyle\geq\sup_{\pi\in\Pi_{\epsilon}}\mathbb{E}_{s}[g_{s}(\pi)]-\varepsilon_{B}-\bar{\varepsilon}_{A}-\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}-2\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}
=Vϵ⋆−εB−ε¯A−δ¯cov−2​ΛH,γ​δϵ.\displaystyle=V_{\epsilon}^{\star}-\varepsilon_{B}-\bar{\varepsilon}_{A}-\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}-2\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}.

□\square

Corollary C.4 (Support-mass interpretation of the coverage gap)

Let

UH:=umax​∑t=1Hγt−1U_{H}:=u_{\max}\sum_{t=1}^{H}\gamma^{t-1} (63)

so that |Js​(π,ω)|≤UH|J_{s}(\pi,\omega)|\leq U_{H} for every rollout execution ω\omega. Assume that a full adversarial best response at the reference planner exists, and let νs,full⋆∈arg⁡minνs∈𝒬s⋆⁡Js​(πref,νs)\nu_{s,\mathrm{full}}^{\star}\in\arg\min_{\nu_{s}\in\mathcal{Q}_{s}^{\star}}J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\nu_{s}). If the infimum is not attained, the same statement holds with an additional arbitrarily small optimality slack by taking an approximate minimizer. Define the omitted support mass

ηs:=νs,full⋆​(Ωs⋆∖Ω^s).\eta_{s}:=\nu_{s,\mathrm{full}}^{\star}\big(\Omega_{s}^{\star}\setminus\widehat{\Omega}_{s}\big). (64)

If ηs<1\eta_{s}<1, then

δcov,s​(πref)≤2​UH​ηs.\delta_{\mathrm{cov},s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})\leq 2U_{H}\eta_{s}. (65)

Consequently,

δ¯cov≤2​UH​𝔼s∼𝒟​[ηs].\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}\leq 2U_{H}\mathbb{E}_{s\sim\mathcal{D}}[\eta_{s}]. (66)

Proof. For each scene ss, let ν~s\widetilde{\nu}_{s} be the renormalization of νs,full⋆\nu_{s,\mathrm{full}}^{\star} onto Ω^s\widehat{\Omega}_{s}. Then ν~s∈𝒬^s\widetilde{\nu}_{s}\in\widehat{\mathcal{Q}}_{s} and

DTV​(ν~s,νs,full⋆)=ηs.D_{\mathrm{TV}}(\widetilde{\nu}_{s},\nu_{s,\mathrm{full}}^{\star})=\eta_{s}. (67)

By the boundedness of the utility,

|Js​(πref,ν~s)−Js​(πref,νs,full⋆)|≤2​UH​ηs.\left|J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\widetilde{\nu}_{s})-J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\nu_{s,\mathrm{full}}^{\star})\right|\leq 2U_{H}\eta_{s}. (68)

Because g^s​(πref)≤Js​(πref,ν~s)\widehat{g}_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})\leq J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\widetilde{\nu}_{s}) and gs​(πref)=Js​(πref,νs,full⋆)g_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})=J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\nu_{s,\mathrm{full}}^{\star}),

δcov,s​(πref)\displaystyle\delta_{\mathrm{cov},s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}) =g^s​(πref)−gs​(πref)\displaystyle=\widehat{g}_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-g_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}) (69)
≤Js​(πref,ν~s)−Js​(πref,νs,full⋆)≤2​UH​ηs.\displaystyle\leq J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\widetilde{\nu}_{s})-J_{s}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}},\nu_{s,\mathrm{full}}^{\star})\leq 2U_{H}\eta_{s}.

Averaging over ss proves Eq. 66. □\square

Interpretation.

Proposition C.2 and Corollaries C.3–C.4 formalize the role of the decoupled Stage-A/B solver. The frozen AWM objective is optimistic relative to the full worst-case game because it evaluates the planner against one learned structured adversary rather than the entire adversary class. However, freezing the AWM is a local Stackelberg approximation whose error is controlled by four interpretable quantities: the Stage-A optimization error ε¯A\bar{\varepsilon}_{A}, the Stage-B best-response error εB\varepsilon_{B}, the structured-class coverage gap δ¯cov\bar{\delta}_{\mathrm{cov}}, and the planner trust-region radius ϵ\epsilon through the 𝒪​(ϵ)\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\epsilon}) term 2​ΛH,γ​δϵ2\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. The support-mass bound further explains why the proposal–top1–pair–calibrator design matters: it should cover the low-utility adversarial executions that carry mass under the true worst-case adversary, while avoiding an intractable dense subset search.

C.3 Scene-Adaptive Pair Admission as a Bayes Gate

The selector and pair ranker decide who should attack. The calibrator decides whether the second attacker should be admitted. This distinction can be formalized as a Bayes decision problem.

For a scene ss, let the candidate pair gain be

Gspair:=Us​({is⋆})−Us​({is⋆,j^s}).G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}:=U_{s}(\{i_{s}^{\star}\})-U_{s}(\{i_{s}^{\star},\widehat{j}_{s}\}). (70)

Positive GspairG_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}} means that the pair attack reduces planner utility more than the top-1 attack. Let ψs\psi_{s} be the scene-level calibrator feature.

Proposition C.5 (Optimal pair-admission rule)

Among all binary admission policies a​(ψs)∈{0,1}a(\psi_{s})\in\{0,1\}, the policy maximizing expected admitted pair gain

𝔼​[a​(ψs)​Gspair]\mathbb{E}\big[a(\psi_{s})G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}\big] (71)

is

a⋆​(ψs)=𝕀​{𝔼​[Gspair∣ψs]>0}.a^{\star}(\psi_{s})=\mathbb{I}\left\{\mathbb{E}[G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}\mid\psi_{s}]>0\right\}. (72)

If the calibrator is calibrated for the binary label

Ys:=𝕀​{Gspair>0},ps=ℙ​(Ys=1∣ψs),Y_{s}:=\mathbb{I}\{G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}>0\},\qquad p_{s}=\mathbb{P}(Y_{s}=1\mid\psi_{s}), (73)

and if

m+​(ψs):=𝔼​[Gspair∣ψs,Ys=1],m−​(ψs):=𝔼​[−Gspair∣ψs,Ys=0],m_{+}(\psi_{s}):=\mathbb{E}[G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}\mid\psi_{s},Y_{s}=1],\qquad m_{-}(\psi_{s}):=\mathbb{E}[-G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}\mid\psi_{s},Y_{s}=0], (74)

then, on feature values where the conditional quantities are well defined and m+​(ψs)+m−​(ψs)>0m_{+}(\psi_{s})+m_{-}(\psi_{s})>0, Eq. 72 is equivalent to

ps>m−​(ψs)m+​(ψs)+m−​(ψs).p_{s}>\frac{m_{-}(\psi_{s})}{m_{+}(\psi_{s})+m_{-}(\psi_{s})}. (75)

When the conditional magnitudes are approximately stable over the deployment distribution, this reduces to a scalar threshold rule ps>τcal⋆p_{s}>\tau_{\mathrm{cal}}^{\star}, which is the tractable rule used by our scene-adaptive calibrator.

Proof. For any measurable admission policy a​(ψs)a(\psi_{s}),

𝔼​[a​(ψs)​Gspair]\displaystyle\mathbb{E}[a(\psi_{s})G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}] =𝔼​[a​(ψs)​𝔼​[Gspair∣ψs]].\displaystyle=\mathbb{E}\left[a(\psi_{s})\mathbb{E}[G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}\mid\psi_{s}]\right]. (76)

Since a​(ψs)a(\psi_{s}) is binary, the pointwise maximizer admits the pair exactly when the conditional expected gain is positive, proving Eq. 72. Next,

𝔼​[Gspair∣ψs]\displaystyle\mathbb{E}[G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}\mid\psi_{s}] =ps​𝔼​[Gspair∣ψs,Ys=1]+(1−ps)​𝔼​[Gspair∣ψs,Ys=0]\displaystyle=p_{s}\mathbb{E}[G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}\mid\psi_{s},Y_{s}=1]+(1-p_{s})\mathbb{E}[G_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}\mid\psi_{s},Y_{s}=0] (77)
=ps​m+​(ψs)−(1−ps)​m−​(ψs).\displaystyle=p_{s}m_{+}(\psi_{s})-(1-p_{s})m_{-}(\psi_{s}).

This quantity is positive if and only if Eq. 75 holds. □\square

Interpretation.

The calibrator is not merely a heuristic confidence score. It is a learned approximation to the Bayes gate that decides whether the additional attack dimension has positive expected value in the current scene. This explains why adaptive K>1K>1 is preferable to fixed K=2K=2: pair attacks should be admitted only when their conditional expected marginal gain is positive.

C.4 Role-Switched Counterfactual Credit as a Difference-Reward Estimator

We next justify the role-switched counterfactual rewards used to train the AWM. For this subsection, fix a scene ss and a factual coalition A=AsfactA=A_{s}^{\mathrm{fact}}. Let Us​(A)U_{s}(A) denote the planner’s utility under the factual coalition, and define the adversarial team reward

Rs​(A):=−Us​(A).R_{s}(A):=-U_{s}(A). (78)

A larger Rs​(A)R_{s}(A) means a stronger attack.

For an active attacker i∈Ai\in A, let Yi=(zi,1,…,zi,H)Y_{i}=(z_{i,1},\ldots,z_{i,H}) denote its sampled factual attack-token trajectory, and let

Ψi:=∇ϕ(i)log⁡pϕ​(Yi∣ℋi,ρi=atk)=∑t=1H∇ϕlog⁡πawm,ϕ​(zi,t∣xt,ρi=atk)\Psi_{i}:=\nabla_{\phi}^{(i)}\log p_{\phi}(Y_{i}\mid\mathcal{H}_{i},\rho_{i}=\texttt{atk})=\sum_{t=1}^{H}\nabla_{\phi}\log\pi_{\mathrm{awm},\phi}\big(z_{i,t}\mid x_{t},\rho_{i}=\texttt{atk}\big) (79)

be the score-function term associated with attacker ii; ℋi\mathcal{H}_{i} denotes the factual pre-sampling history needed to define the conditional trajectory law. When parameters are shared across attackers, the full attacker-side policy-gradient estimator sums Eq. 79 over active attackers.

Let bg​(A)\mathrm{bg}(A) denote the counterfactual rollout obtained by switching all active attackers in AA to the background role without removing the agents from the scene. Define the admissible baselines

Biloo:=−Us​(A∖{i}),Bteam:=−Us​(bg​(A)),B_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}:=-U_{s}(A\setminus\{i\}),\qquad B^{\mathrm{team}}:=-U_{s}(\mathrm{bg}(A)), (80)

where both quantities are evaluated by the action-independent counterfactual replay described above. Then

Δiloo=Us​(A∖{i})−Us​(A)=Rs​(A)−Biloo,\Delta_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}=U_{s}(A\setminus\{i\})-U_{s}(A)=R_{s}(A)-B_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}, (81)

and

Δsteam=Us​(bg​(A))−Us​(A)=Rs​(A)−Bteam.\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{team}}=U_{s}(\mathrm{bg}(A))-U_{s}(A)=R_{s}(A)-B^{\mathrm{team}}. (82)
Proposition C.6 (Hybrid counterfactual reward is gradient-aligned)

Assume the counterfactual utilities used to form BilooB_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}} and BteamB^{\mathrm{team}} are evaluated with stop-gradient, are ℱicf\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}}-measurable, and are conditionally independent of the factual attacker trajectory YiY_{i} given ℱicf\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}}. For weights ωloo,ωteam≥0\omega_{\mathrm{loo}},\omega_{\mathrm{team}}\geq 0, define

Δihyb:=ωloo​Δiloo+ωteam​Δsteam,W:=ωloo+ωteam.\Delta_{i}^{\mathrm{hyb}}:=\omega_{\mathrm{loo}}\Delta_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}+\omega_{\mathrm{team}}\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{team}},\qquad W:=\omega_{\mathrm{loo}}+\omega_{\mathrm{team}}. (83)

Then

𝔼​[Ψi​Δihyb]=W​∇ϕ(i)𝔼​[Rs​(A)],\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}\Delta_{i}^{\mathrm{hyb}}]=W\,\nabla_{\phi}^{(i)}\mathbb{E}[R_{s}(A)], (84)

where ∇ϕ(i)\nabla_{\phi}^{(i)} denotes the policy-gradient contribution associated with attacker ii. In particular, if W>0W>0, the hybrid counterfactual reward has the same ascent direction as the adversarial team objective up to the positive scalar WW; if W=1W=1, it is an unbiased estimator of that component.

Proof. By the score-function identity applied to the factual attack trajectory,

∇ϕ(i)𝔼​[Rs​(A)]=𝔼​[Ψi​Rs​(A)].\nabla_{\phi}^{(i)}\mathbb{E}[R_{s}(A)]=\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}R_{s}(A)]. (85)

It remains to show that the counterfactual baselines vanish after multiplication by Ψi\Psi_{i}. Because BilooB_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}} is ℱicf\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}}-measurable and conditionally independent of YiY_{i} given ℱicf\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}},

𝔼​[Ψi​Biloo]\displaystyle\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}B_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}] =𝔼​[Biloo​𝔼​[Ψi∣ℱicf]].\displaystyle=\mathbb{E}\big[B_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}\mid\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}}]\big]. (86)

For the conditional score,

𝔼​[Ψi∣ℱicf]\displaystyle\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}\mid\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}}] =∑yipϕ​(yi∣ℱicf)​∇ϕlog⁡pϕ​(yi∣ℱicf)\displaystyle=\sum_{y_{i}}p_{\phi}(y_{i}\mid\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}})\nabla_{\phi}\log p_{\phi}(y_{i}\mid\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}}) (87)
=∑yi∇ϕpϕ​(yi∣ℱicf)=∇ϕ​∑yipϕ​(yi∣ℱicf)=0.\displaystyle=\sum_{y_{i}}\nabla_{\phi}p_{\phi}(y_{i}\mid\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}})=\nabla_{\phi}\sum_{y_{i}}p_{\phi}(y_{i}\mid\mathcal{F}_{i}^{\mathrm{cf}})=0.

Thus 𝔼​[Ψi​Biloo]=0\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}B_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}]=0. The same argument gives 𝔼​[Ψi​Bteam]=0\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}B^{\mathrm{team}}]=0. Therefore,

𝔼​[Ψi​Δihyb]\displaystyle\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}\Delta_{i}^{\mathrm{hyb}}] =ωloo​𝔼​[Ψi​(Rs​(A)−Biloo)]+ωteam​𝔼​[Ψi​(Rs​(A)−Bteam)]\displaystyle=\omega_{\mathrm{loo}}\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}(R_{s}(A)-B_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}})]+\omega_{\mathrm{team}}\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}(R_{s}(A)-B^{\mathrm{team}})] (88)
=(ωloo+ωteam)​𝔼​[Ψi​Rs​(A)]=W​∇ϕ(i)𝔼​[Rs​(A)].\displaystyle=(\omega_{\mathrm{loo}}+\omega_{\mathrm{team}})\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}R_{s}(A)]=W\,\nabla_{\phi}^{(i)}\mathbb{E}[R_{s}(A)].

□\square

Lemma C.7 (Variance-optimal action-independent baseline)

Let ℱ−i\mathcal{F}_{-i} be any sigma-field that excludes the factual attacker sample YiY_{i} and satisfies 𝔼​[Ψi∣ℱ−i]=0\mathbb{E}[\Psi_{i}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}]=0. Consider any ℱ−i\mathcal{F}_{-i}-measurable baseline bi​(ℱ−i)b_{i}(\mathcal{F}_{-i}). Define the score-weighted second moment

𝒱​(bi):=𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22​(Rs​(A)−bi​(ℱ−i))2].\mathcal{V}(b_{i}):=\mathbb{E}\left[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}\big(R_{s}(A)-b_{i}(\mathcal{F}_{-i})\big)^{2}\right]. (89)

On events where 𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22∣ℱ−i]>0\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}]>0, the unique minimizer among all ℱ−i\mathcal{F}_{-i}-measurable baselines is

bi⋆​(ℱ−i)=𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22​Rs​(A)∣ℱ−i]𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22∣ℱ−i].b_{i}^{\star}(\mathcal{F}_{-i})=\frac{\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}R_{s}(A)\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}]}{\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}]}. (90)

On events where this conditional second moment is zero, the choice of baseline is immaterial. Moreover, for any other baseline bib_{i},

𝒱​(bi)=𝒱​(bi⋆)+𝔼​[𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22∣ℱ−i]​(bi​(ℱ−i)−bi⋆​(ℱ−i))2].\mathcal{V}(b_{i})=\mathcal{V}(b_{i}^{\star})+\mathbb{E}\left[\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}]\big(b_{i}(\mathcal{F}_{-i})-b_{i}^{\star}(\mathcal{F}_{-i})\big)^{2}\right]. (91)

If 𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22∣ℱ−i,Rs​(A)]=𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22∣ℱ−i]\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i},R_{s}(A)]=\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}], then

bi⋆​(ℱ−i)=𝔼​[Rs​(A)∣ℱ−i].b_{i}^{\star}(\mathcal{F}_{-i})=\mathbb{E}[R_{s}(A)\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}]. (92)

Proof. Condition on ℱ−i\mathcal{F}_{-i}. For any scalar value bi=bi​(ℱ−i)b_{i}=b_{i}(\mathcal{F}_{-i}), the conditional objective is

𝒱​(bi∣ℱ−i)\displaystyle\mathcal{V}(b_{i}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}) =𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22​Rs​(A)2∣ℱ−i]−2​bi​𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22​Rs​(A)∣ℱ−i]\displaystyle=\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}R_{s}(A)^{2}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}]-2b_{i}\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}R_{s}(A)\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}] (93)
+bi2​𝔼​[‖Ψi‖22∣ℱ−i].\displaystyle\quad+b_{i}^{2}\mathbb{E}[\|\Psi_{i}\|_{2}^{2}\mid\mathcal{F}_{-i}].

This is a quadratic in bib_{i}. Differentiating and setting the derivative to zero gives Eq. 90. Completing the square yields Eq. 91. If the score magnitude is conditionally independent of the reward given ℱ−i\mathcal{F}_{-i}, the numerator in Eq. 90 factorizes, giving Eq. 92. □\square

Remark C.8 (Pairwise Shapley reconstruction)

When the admitted coalition has size two, A={i,j}A=\{i,j\}, define the cooperative game

v​(B):=Us​(bg​(A))−Us​(B),B⊆A,v(B):=U_{s}(\mathrm{bg}(A))-U_{s}(B),\qquad B\subseteq A, (94)

so that v​(∅)=0v(\emptyset)=0 and v​(A)=Δsteamv(A)=\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{team}}. The Shapley values of the two attackers are

ϕi\displaystyle\phi_{i} =12​(v​({i})−v​(∅))+12​(v​({i,j})−v​({j})),\displaystyle=\frac{1}{2}\big(v(\{i\})-v(\emptyset)\big)+\frac{1}{2}\big(v(\{i,j\})-v(\{j\})\big), (95)
ϕj\displaystyle\phi_{j} =12​(v​({j})−v​(∅))+12​(v​({i,j})−v​({i})).\displaystyle=\frac{1}{2}\big(v(\{j\})-v(\emptyset)\big)+\frac{1}{2}\big(v(\{i,j\})-v(\{i\})\big).

Using

Δiloo=v​({i,j})−v​({j}),Δjloo=v​({i,j})−v​({i}),\Delta_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}=v(\{i,j\})-v(\{j\}),\qquad\Delta_{j}^{\mathrm{loo}}=v(\{i,j\})-v(\{i\}), (96)

we obtain

ϕi=12​(Δsteam+Δiloo−Δjloo),ϕj=12​(Δsteam+Δjloo−Δiloo).\phi_{i}=\frac{1}{2}\big(\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{team}}+\Delta_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}-\Delta_{j}^{\mathrm{loo}}\big),\qquad\phi_{j}=\frac{1}{2}\big(\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{team}}+\Delta_{j}^{\mathrm{loo}}-\Delta_{i}^{\mathrm{loo}}\big). (97)

Thus, in the Kmax=2K_{\max}=2 regime used by our method, the team and leave-one-out counterfactuals span the exact fair-credit decomposition of the corresponding pairwise cooperative game.

Interpretation.

Proposition C.6 and Lemma C.7 show that role-switched counterfactual rewards are not arbitrary heuristics. They are difference-reward control variates for score-function optimization [16, 14] under the action-independence condition stated above. Because the factual and counterfactual rollouts share the same model, token vocabulary, transition operator, and scene context, the baselines remove nuisance scene variation; because the counterfactual utility is detached and independent of the factual attacker sample, the attacker-side score-function direction is preserved. The team term further preserves non-additive coalition information, which is essential for learning cooperative pair attacks.

C.5 Regret as a Reference-Anchored Control Variate

We now analyze the planner-side robust objective. Stage B freezes the learned AWM and optimizes the planner against the induced top1/pair branch distribution. Throughout this subsection, the branch identity rule and the calibrated probability psp_{s} are treated as frozen with respect to the planner parameters. Let

Jsb​(π),b∈{top1,pair},J_{s}^{b}(\pi),\qquad b\in\{\mathrm{top1},\mathrm{pair}\}, (98)

denote the planner utility in scene ss under a frozen adversarial branch bb. We define the reference-relative adversarial regret as

Δsb​(θ):=Jsb​(πref)−Jsb​(πθ).\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta):=J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta}). (99)

A positive value means that the current planner underperforms the reference planner under the same attack.

Proposition C.9 (Regret preserves branchwise gradient direction and reduces scene difficulty)

For any branch bb,

∇θ𝔼s​[Δsb​(θ)]=−∇θ𝔼s​[Jsb​(πθ)].\nabla_{\theta}\mathbb{E}_{s}[\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta)]=-\nabla_{\theta}\mathbb{E}_{s}[J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta})]. (100)

Thus, minimizing regret is branchwise equivalent to maximizing utility. Moreover,

Vars​[Δsb​(θ)]\displaystyle\mathrm{Var}_{s}[\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta)] =Vars​[Jsb​(πθ)]+Vars​[Jsb​(πref)]\displaystyle=\mathrm{Var}_{s}[J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta})]+\mathrm{Var}_{s}[J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})]
−2​C​o​vs​(Jsb​(πθ),Jsb​(πref)).\displaystyle\quad-2\mathrm{Cov}_{s}\big(J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta}),J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})\big). (101)

Consequently,

Vars​[Δsb​(θ)]≤Vars​[Jsb​(πθ)]\mathrm{Var}_{s}[\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta)]\leq\mathrm{Var}_{s}[J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta})] (102)

whenever

2​C​o​vs​(Jsb​(πθ),Jsb​(πref))≥Vars​[Jsb​(πref)].2\mathrm{Cov}_{s}\big(J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta}),J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})\big)\geq\mathrm{Var}_{s}[J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})]. (103)

Finally, if πθ∈Πϵ\pi_{\theta}\in\Pi_{\epsilon}, then

|Δsb​(θ)|≤ΛH,γ​δϵ,Vars​[Δsb​(θ)]≤ΛH,γ2​δϵ2.|\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta)|\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon},\qquad\mathrm{Var}_{s}[\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta)]\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}^{2}\delta_{\epsilon}^{2}. (104)

Proof. Since πref\pi_{\mathrm{ref}} is frozen,

∇θΔsb​(θ)=−∇θJsb​(πθ),\nabla_{\theta}\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta)=-\nabla_{\theta}J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta}), (105)

which proves Eq. 100 after taking expectation over scenes. Eq. 101 follows from

Var​(Y−X)=Var​(Y)+Var​(X)−2​C​o​v​(X,Y),\mathrm{Var}(Y-X)=\mathrm{Var}(Y)+\mathrm{Var}(X)-2\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y), (106)

with Y=Jsb​(πref)Y=J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}) and X=Jsb​(πθ)X=J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta}). The sufficient condition in Eq. 103 follows immediately.

For Eq. 104, apply Lemma C.1 to the fixed branch environment:

|Δsb​(θ)|=|Jsb​(πref)−Jsb​(πθ)|≤ΛH,γ​δ​(πθ,πref)≤ΛH,γ​δϵ.|\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta)|=|J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta})|\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta(\pi_{\theta},\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. (107)

A random variable bounded in absolute value by cc has variance at most c2c^{2}, yielding the variance bound. □\square

Remark C.10 (Generalized control-variate coefficient)

More generally, define

Δs,βb​(θ):=β​Jsb​(πref)−Jsb​(πθ).\Delta_{s,\beta}^{b}(\theta):=\beta J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta}). (108)

For every β∈ℝ\beta\in\mathbb{R},

∇θ𝔼s​[Δs,βb​(θ)]=−∇θ𝔼s​[Jsb​(πθ)].\nabla_{\theta}\mathbb{E}_{s}[\Delta_{s,\beta}^{b}(\theta)]=-\nabla_{\theta}\mathbb{E}_{s}[J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta})]. (109)

When Vars​(Jsb​(πref))>0\mathrm{Var}_{s}(J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}))>0, the variance-minimizing coefficient is

β⋆=Covs​(Jsb​(πθ),Jsb​(πref))Vars​(Jsb​(πref)).\beta^{\star}=\frac{\mathrm{Cov}_{s}(J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta}),J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}))}{\mathrm{Var}_{s}(J_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}))}. (110)

Our implementation uses the simple unit coefficient β=1\beta=1, which is natural when πθ\pi_{\theta} remains close to πref\pi_{\mathrm{ref}} under the trust region and the two branch utilities are highly correlated.

Interpretation.

Regret does not change the branchwise optimization direction. It changes the cross-scene random variable to which the tail-risk operator is applied. In autonomous driving, absolute utility can be dominated by scene-intrinsic difficulty. If

Jsb​(π)=dsb+J~sb​(π),J_{s}^{b}(\pi)=d_{s}^{b}+\widetilde{J}_{s}^{b}(\pi), (111)

where dsbd_{s}^{b} is independent of the planner, then

Δsb​(θ)=J~sb​(πref)−J~sb​(πθ),\Delta_{s}^{b}(\theta)=\widetilde{J}_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-\widetilde{J}_{s}^{b}(\pi_{\theta}), (112)

so the intrinsic difficulty term cancels exactly. This explains why CVaR should be applied to regret rather than absolute utility: absolute-utility CVaR can overemphasize intrinsically hard scenes, whereas regret-CVaR focuses on planner-induced degradation relative to the same reference policy.

C.6 Closed-Form CVaR on the AWM-Induced Two-Point Risk Distribution

For each scene, the frozen AWM induces a two-point adversarial branch distribution:

𝒟sθ={(Δstop1​(θ),1−ps),(Δspair​(θ),ps)},\mathcal{D}_{s}^{\theta}=\left\{\big(\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{top1}}(\theta),1-p_{s}\big),\big(\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}(\theta),p_{s}\big)\right\}, (113)

where psp_{s} is the calibrated pair-admission probability and is frozen during the planner update. We use upper-tail CVaR with tail mass τ∈(0,1]\tau\in(0,1]:

CVaRτ​(Z):=minη∈ℝ⁡{η+1τ​𝔼​[(Z−η)+]}.\mathrm{CVaR}_{\tau}(Z):=\min_{\eta\in\mathbb{R}}\left\{\eta+\frac{1}{\tau}\mathbb{E}[(Z-\eta)_{+}]\right\}. (114)

This is the standard Rockafellar–Uryasev variational form for upper-tail risk [35], written with tail mass τ\tau.

Proposition C.11 (Closed-form CVaR weights and rare-risk amplification)

Let the two branch regrets be sorted as

Δsb(1)​(θ)≥Δsb(2)​(θ),\Delta_{s}^{b_{(1)}}(\theta)\geq\Delta_{s}^{b_{(2)}}(\theta), (115)

and let μsb(1)∈{ps,1−ps}\mu_{s}^{b_{(1)}}\in\{p_{s},1-p_{s}\} be the probability mass of the worse branch. Then

CVaRτ​(𝒟sθ)=wsb(1)​(τ)​Δsb(1)​(θ)+wsb(2)​(τ)​Δsb(2)​(θ),\mathrm{CVaR}_{\tau}(\mathcal{D}_{s}^{\theta})=w_{s}^{b_{(1)}}(\tau)\Delta_{s}^{b_{(1)}}(\theta)+w_{s}^{b_{(2)}}(\tau)\Delta_{s}^{b_{(2)}}(\theta), (116)

where

wsb(1)​(τ)={1,μsb(1)≥τ,μsb(1)τ,μsb(1)<τ,wsb(2)​(τ)=1−wsb(1)​(τ).w_{s}^{b_{(1)}}(\tau)=\begin{cases}1,&\mu_{s}^{b_{(1)}}\geq\tau,\\[4.0pt] \dfrac{\mu_{s}^{b_{(1)}}}{\tau},&\mu_{s}^{b_{(1)}}<\tau,\end{cases}\qquad w_{s}^{b_{(2)}}(\tau)=1-w_{s}^{b_{(1)}}(\tau). (117)

Away from the tie set Δstop1=Δspair\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{top1}}=\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}, the subgradient is

∇θCVaRτ​(𝒟sθ)=wstop1​(τ)​∇θΔstop1​(θ)+wspair​(τ)​∇θΔspair​(θ).\nabla_{\theta}\mathrm{CVaR}_{\tau}(\mathcal{D}_{s}^{\theta})=w_{s}^{\mathrm{top1}}(\tau)\nabla_{\theta}\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{top1}}(\theta)+w_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}(\tau)\nabla_{\theta}\Delta_{s}^{\mathrm{pair}}(\theta). (118)

If the pair branch is the worse branch and ps<τp_{s}<\tau, then its CVaR coefficient is ps/τp_{s}/\tau. Compared with ordinary expected regret, where the coefficient is psp_{s}, the pair branch receives the amplification factor

ps/τps=1τ.\frac{p_{s}/\tau}{p_{s}}=\frac{1}{\tau}. (119)

Proof. Let z(1)≥z(2)z_{(1)}\geq z_{(2)} denote the two sorted regret values and let μ\mu be the probability mass of z(1)z_{(1)}. For η∈ℝ\eta\in\mathbb{R}, define

f​(η):=η+1τ​[μ​(z(1)−η)++(1−μ)​(z(2)−η)+].f(\eta):=\eta+\frac{1}{\tau}\left[\mu(z_{(1)}-\eta)_{+}+(1-\mu)(z_{(2)}-\eta)_{+}\right]. (120)

We analyze three regions.

If η≥z(1)\eta\geq z_{(1)}, both hinge terms vanish and f​(η)=ηf(\eta)=\eta, minimized at η=z(1)\eta=z_{(1)} with value z(1)z_{(1)}.

If z(2)≤η≤z(1)z_{(2)}\leq\eta\leq z_{(1)}, only the first hinge is active:

f​(η)=η+μτ​(z(1)−η)=μτ​z(1)+(1−μτ)​η.f(\eta)=\eta+\frac{\mu}{\tau}(z_{(1)}-\eta)=\frac{\mu}{\tau}z_{(1)}+\left(1-\frac{\mu}{\tau}\right)\eta. (121)

If μ≥τ\mu\geq\tau, the slope is non-positive, so the minimum over this interval is attained at η=z(1)\eta=z_{(1)}, giving value z(1)z_{(1)}. If μ<τ\mu<\tau, the slope is positive, so the minimum is attained at η=z(2)\eta=z_{(2)}, giving

μτ​z(1)+(1−μτ)​z(2).\frac{\mu}{\tau}z_{(1)}+\left(1-\frac{\mu}{\tau}\right)z_{(2)}. (122)

If η≤z(2)\eta\leq z_{(2)}, both hinge terms are active:

f​(η)\displaystyle f(\eta) =η+1τ​[μ​(z(1)−η)+(1−μ)​(z(2)−η)]\displaystyle=\eta+\frac{1}{\tau}\left[\mu(z_{(1)}-\eta)+(1-\mu)(z_{(2)}-\eta)\right] (123)
=μτ​z(1)+1−μτ​z(2)+(1−1τ)​η.\displaystyle=\frac{\mu}{\tau}z_{(1)}+\frac{1-\mu}{\tau}z_{(2)}+\left(1-\frac{1}{\tau}\right)\eta.

Since τ∈(0,1]\tau\in(0,1], the slope is non-positive, so the minimum over this region is attained at the largest feasible η\eta, namely η=z(2)\eta=z_{(2)}. This gives the same value as the previous case when μ<τ\mu<\tau. Combining the cases proves Eq. 116 and Eq. 117. The subgradient expression follows by differentiating the piecewise-linear closed form away from branch ties. The amplification claim follows by setting μ=ps\mu=p_{s} when the pair branch is worse and ps<τp_{s}<\tau. □\square

Interpretation.

The calibrated Pair attacks are intentionally sparse: in most scenes, the calibrated probability psp_{s} may be small. Expected regret would multiply the pair-branch gradient by psp_{s}, causing rare but severe cooperative failures to be under-optimized. CVaR prevents this dilution. If the rare pair branch is currently the worst branch, it is amplified by exactly 1/τ1/\tau until it either no longer dominates the tail or its probability mass saturates the entire tail. This is why the planner-side objective is regret-sensitive and CVaR-oriented rather than a simple expectation over AWM samples.

In the implementation, the CVaR weights are treated with stop-gradient. On every region where the branch ordering is fixed, this coincides with one valid subgradient of the piecewise-linear CVaR objective in Eq. 118. At branch ties, the CVaR subdifferential is set-valued; any convex combination of the tied-branch gradients is valid.

C.7 Nominal Retention as a Local Performance Floor

We finally analyze the nominal-retention and safety-constraint mechanisms. Let

Jsnorm​(π):=Us​(π,πpred)J_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi):=U_{s}(\pi,\pi_{\mathrm{pred}}) (124)

be the scene-level nominal utility, and define

Jnorm​(π):=𝔼s∼𝒟​[Jsnorm​(π)].J^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi):=\mathbb{E}_{s\sim\mathcal{D}}[J_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi)]. (125)

Recall the normal utility margin

msnorm​(θ):=Jsnorm​(πθ)−Jsnorm​(πref)m_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta):=J_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\theta})-J_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}) (126)

and the asymmetric penalty

ψsnorm​(θ):=[−ϵnorm−msnorm​(θ)]+2.\psi_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta):=\left[-\epsilon_{\mathrm{norm}}-m_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)\right]_{+}^{2}. (127)
Proposition C.12 (Nominal performance floor)

For every πθ∈Πϵ\pi_{\theta}\in\Pi_{\epsilon} and every scene ss,

|Jsnorm​(πθ)−Jsnorm​(πref)|≤ΛH,γ​δϵ.\big|J_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\theta})-J_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})\big|\leq\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon}. (128)

Moreover, the asymmetric margin penalty implies the pointwise bound

msnorm​(θ)≥−ϵnorm−ψsnorm​(θ).m_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)\geq-\epsilon_{\mathrm{norm}}-\sqrt{\psi_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)}. (129)

Taking expectation over scenes gives

Jnorm​(πθ)≥Jnorm​(πref)−ϵnorm−𝔼s​[ψsnorm​(θ)].J^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\theta})\geq J^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-\epsilon_{\mathrm{norm}}-\sqrt{\mathbb{E}_{s}[\psi_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)]}. (130)

Therefore,

Jnorm​(πθ)≥Jnorm​(πref)−min⁡{ΛH,γ​δϵ,ϵnorm+𝔼s​[ψsnorm​(θ)]}.J^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\theta})\geq J^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-\min\left\{\Lambda_{H,\gamma}\delta_{\epsilon},\;\epsilon_{\mathrm{norm}}+\sqrt{\mathbb{E}_{s}[\psi_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)]}\right\}. (131)

In particular, if ψsnorm​(θ)=0\psi_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)=0 almost surely, then

Jsnorm​(πθ)≥Jsnorm​(πref)−ϵnormfor almost every scene ​s.J_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\theta})\geq J_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}})-\epsilon_{\mathrm{norm}}\qquad\text{for almost every scene }s. (132)

Proof. Eq. 128 is Lemma C.1 applied to the nominal environment πpred\pi_{\mathrm{pred}}. For the margin bound, define

as​(θ):=[−ϵnorm−msnorm​(θ)]+=ψsnorm​(θ).a_{s}(\theta):=\left[-\epsilon_{\mathrm{norm}}-m_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)\right]_{+}=\sqrt{\psi_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)}. (133)

Since x≤[x]+x\leq[x]_{+} for all real xx,

−ϵnorm−msnorm​(θ)≤as​(θ),-\epsilon_{\mathrm{norm}}-m_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)\leq a_{s}(\theta), (134)

which rearranges to Eq. 129. Taking expectations,

Jnorm​(πθ)−Jnorm​(πref)\displaystyle J^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\theta})-J^{\mathrm{norm}}(\pi_{\mathrm{ref}}) =𝔼s​[msnorm​(θ)]\displaystyle=\mathbb{E}_{s}[m_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)] (135)
≥−ϵnorm−𝔼s​[as​(θ)].\displaystyle\geq-\epsilon_{\mathrm{norm}}-\mathbb{E}_{s}[a_{s}(\theta)]. (136)

By Jensen’s inequality,

𝔼s​[as​(θ)]≤𝔼s​[ψsnorm​(θ)],\mathbb{E}_{s}[a_{s}(\theta)]\leq\sqrt{\mathbb{E}_{s}[\psi_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)]}, (137)

which proves Eq. 130. Both Eq. 128 and Eq. 130 are valid lower bounds on expected nominal utility, so taking the tighter one gives Eq. 131. If ψsnorm​(θ)=0\psi_{s}^{\mathrm{norm}}(\theta)=0, Eq. 129 reduces to Eq. 132. □\square

Interpretation.

Proposition C.12 is the finite-horizon autoregressive analogue of trust-region and conservative policy-improvement arguments [22, 37]. The KL anchor limits how far the planner can move from the reference behavior, while the asymmetric margin penalty directly controls degradation in normal traffic. Thus robustness is not obtained by unconstrained overfitting to adversarial samples; it is obtained by a controlled local update with an explicit nominal-performance floor.

C.8 Safety Dual Variables as a Projected Max-Violation Surrogate

Finally, we analyze the safety dual update in Section 3.3. For each safety channel mm, define the branch-mixed violation gap

hm​(θ):=𝔼s∼𝒟​[v¯s,m]−κm,v¯s,m:=(1−ps)​vs,mtop1+ps​vs,mpair.h_{m}(\theta):=\mathbb{E}_{s\sim\mathcal{D}}[\bar{v}_{s,m}]-\kappa_{m},\qquad\bar{v}_{s,m}:=(1-p_{s})v_{s,m}^{\mathrm{top1}}+p_{s}v_{s,m}^{\mathrm{pair}}. (138)

In a classical Lagrangian relaxation, one would use nonnegative multipliers λm≥0\lambda_{m}\geq 0 and maximize ∑mλm​hm​(θ)\sum_{m}\lambda_{m}h_{m}(\theta). Our implementation instead uses a bounded softmax parameterization

[λR,λ1,…,λM]=softmax⁡([αR,ζ1,…,ζM]),[\lambda_{R},\lambda_{1},\ldots,\lambda_{M}]=\operatorname{softmax}([\alpha_{R},\zeta_{1},\ldots,\zeta_{M}]), (139)

and maximizes

ℒdual​(ζ)=∑m=1Mλm​(ζ)​hm​(θ).\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{dual}}(\zeta)=\sum_{m=1}^{M}\lambda_{m}(\zeta)h_{m}(\theta). (140)

For analysis, define hR:=0h_{R}:=0 for the progress channel.

Proposition C.13 (Softmax safety multipliers as a bounded max-violation surrogate)

For fixed planner parameters θ\theta, the gradient of Eq. 140 with respect to the safety logit ζm\zeta_{m} is

∂ℒdual∂ζm=λm​(hm−∑ℓ=1Mλℓ​hℓ).\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{dual}}}{\partial\zeta_{m}}=\lambda_{m}\left(h_{m}-\sum_{\ell=1}^{M}\lambda_{\ell}h_{\ell}\right). (141)

Thus dual ascent increases the relative weight of safety channels whose violation gap exceeds the current softmax-weighted average, and decreases the relative weight of channels below that average. Moreover,

supζ∈ℝMℒdual​(ζ)=max⁡{0,maxm∈{1,…,M}⁡hm​(θ)}.\sup_{\zeta\in\mathbb{R}^{M}}\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{dual}}(\zeta)=\max\left\{0,\max_{m\in\{1,\ldots,M\}}h_{m}(\theta)\right\}. (142)

Proof. The softmax derivative gives

∂λℓ∂ζm=λℓ​(𝕀​{ℓ=m}−λm),ℓ∈{1,…,M}.\frac{\partial\lambda_{\ell}}{\partial\zeta_{m}}=\lambda_{\ell}(\mathbb{I}\{\ell=m\}-\lambda_{m}),\qquad\ell\in\{1,\ldots,M\}. (143)

Therefore,

∂ℒdual∂ζm\displaystyle\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{dual}}}{\partial\zeta_{m}} =∑ℓ=1Mhℓ​∂λℓ∂ζm=λm​hm−λm​∑ℓ=1Mλℓ​hℓ\displaystyle=\sum_{\ell=1}^{M}h_{\ell}\frac{\partial\lambda_{\ell}}{\partial\zeta_{m}}=\lambda_{m}h_{m}-\lambda_{m}\sum_{\ell=1}^{M}\lambda_{\ell}h_{\ell} (144)
=λm​(hm−∑ℓ=1Mλℓ​hℓ).\displaystyle=\lambda_{m}\left(h_{m}-\sum_{\ell=1}^{M}\lambda_{\ell}h_{\ell}\right).

This proves Eq. 141.

For Eq. 142, note that the softmax weights range over the relative interior of the simplex over the MM safety channels plus the progress channel with value hR=0h_{R}=0. Thus ℒdual\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{dual}} is a convex combination of {0,h1,…,hM}\{0,h_{1},\ldots,h_{M}\} and can approach, but not necessarily attain at finite logits, the maximum of these values. Therefore the supremum is max⁡{0,maxm⁡hm}\max\{0,\max_{m}h_{m}\}. □\square

Appendix D Supplementary Results

This section provides the complete empirical evidence underlying Section 4. We first report the InterPlan-LongTail breakdown and simulator-side transfer diagnostics, and then give detailed analyses of planner-world cross-validation, AWM mechanisms, planner adaptation, adapted prior generators, and qualitative visualizations.

D.1 Closed-Loop Long-Tail Breakdown

Table 5 decomposes the InterPlan-LongTail score by scenario template. Scores use the same percentage scale as Table 1. The per-template breakdown shows that AWM-Planner improves over Plan-R1 on seven of eight templates. The largest gains appear in close straight-driving jaywalker and medium-density yielding cases, which are precisely the settings where earlier recovery from interactive conflicts is valuable. The only negative row is left_turn_yield, where the difference is small. Thus, the long-tail result shows that AWM improves a broad set of recoverable interaction failures.

D.2 Adversarial Sim-Agent Diagnostics

Table 6 reports the simulator-side diagnostics used to examine the AWM sim-agent interface described in Appendix B.4. The fallback rate is the fraction of proposed sim-agent updates that revert to the normal policy after feasibility checks. The ego-gate rate measures how often the stricter ego-local guard suppresses an adversarial proposal. The active-adversary count measures the coalition size under the attack budget Kmax=2K_{\max}=2, and budget utilization is the active-adversary count divided by KmaxK_{\max}. These diagnostics complement the score table in the main text: AWM consistently uses nearly the full two-agent budget, but its high fallback rate shows that the adversarial host is substantially constrained by the feasibility gate. The ego-local gate remains low across planners, indicating that the observed score drop is mainly caused by feasible interaction pressure rather than by frequent emergency suppression around the ego.

D.3 Planner-World Cross-Validation Details

Table 7 reports the full planner and world matrix summarized in Figure 2(a). The post-trained planner improves under both the normal and adversarial worlds, but the increase is larger under AWM, especially for Tail-CVaR. This confirms that Stage B does not merely shift the planner toward a different nominal policy. It specifically improves the lower tail exposed by the adversarial model.

Table 8 decomposes the adversarial branch used in the planner-world cross-validation of Section 4. The training host produces moderate drops of 0.0120.012–0.0200.020, while the held-out AWM increases the drops to 0.0250.025–0.0400.040. Pair and Adaptive branches expose larger lower-tail risk than the single-agent Top-1 branch, with Adaptive remaining less aggressive than forced Pair because it controls the behavior through scene-level admission. This pattern supports the use of calibrated coalition admission in the training risk distribution.

D.4 AWM Mechanism Ablations

Tables 9–12 provide the detailed mechanism evidence for the AWM design summarized in Section 4. The credit-assignment ablation in Table 9 shows that role-conditioned counterfactual credit gives stronger tail stress than coarse global reward or assignment-only variants while avoiding the severe trajectory-fidelity collapse of coarse baselines.

The coalition ablations in Tables 10 and 11 separate raw attack strength from pair-admission control. Forced K=2K{=}2 and no-calibrator variants can create stronger raw stress, but they admit pair attacks in most scenes and degrade behavior realism. The adaptive calibrated variant keeps pair usage sparse while retaining most of the sim-agent attack effect. These results show that pair attacks are useful but should be selected conditionally.

Table 12 further tests whether adversarial stress arises from coherent role-conditioned rollouts. Post-hoc replacement or injection preserves more nominal behavior and can appear favorable under simple ADE/FDE metrics, but it produces much weaker tail stress and attack strength. The full role-conditioned AWM therefore provides the strongest coherent attack while remaining realistic.

D.5 Additional Planner Ablations

Table 13 evaluates the sensitivity of Stage B to the frozen opponent used inside the inference-time rollout. The matched self-play AWM gives the best combined results. Training with a nominal world model or a held-out external AWM remains functional, but the resulting planner is weaker. This indicates that the learned opponent distribution is most effective when the planner is optimized against the same calibrated AWM used to define the adversarial risk distribution.

Table 14 and Figure 5 report hyperparameter sensitivity. The main trend is a trade-off between robustness and nominal performance: stronger tail-risk pressure or lower admission thresholds can improve adversarial tail metrics, but overly aggressive settings reduce closed-loop performance. Stronger retention preserves nominal behavior but can under-emphasize adversarial recovery.

D.6 Adapted Prior Adversarial Generators

Table 15 gives the full adapted generator comparison summarized in Figure 2(b). The comparison separates adversarial strength from realism and search budget by adapting prior generators to the same autoregressive rollout interface. Single-candidate CAT∗ and STRIVE∗ produce relatively weak tail stress, while best-4 search strengthens all generators by selecting more damaging samples. The KING-style aggressive bound obtains the largest raw drop, but at a substantial cost in ADE/FDE and background-collision proxies.

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.
